unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Number and Quantity: Matching the Number to a Concrete Quantity.
text:
DAY 1. 
Lesson Title: Number and Quantity: Matching the Number to a Concrete Quantity. 
Goal: Students inspect a familiar classroom situation with objects, balls, and seats, then decide that a number is 
not accepted unless it matches a small visible or touchable quantity suitable for the child’s readiness. 
Tools: Safe cubes, small balls, toy seats, paper plates, simple quantity pictures, small number cards, a short 
floor number line, and small evidence cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters carrying an almost-empty biscuit box and shakes it as if it were a treasure, then 
whispers, “The box says there are 7 inside… should I believe it?” The teacher opens the lid for one quick 
moment so the class sees a mismatch between the card and what is inside, then points to the eyes and says, 
“Today our eyes and hands will catch the lying number.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a full group facing a number card on a box 
that does not match what is inside; students then take clear roles: the toucher, the counter, the matcher, and the 
trust-or-do-not-trust speaker. For support, the teacher begins with quantities from 1 to 3; for children ready for 
extension, the quantity may be slightly larger without making counting to 20 or 30 a general requirement for all. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that a number in school mathematics is not only a sound we say or 
a card we hold; it is a symbol that represents a quantity. Today’s academic concept is number and quantity with 
one-to-one correspondence: every object is counted once, and the number becomes correct only when it 
matches a concrete quantity the child can see or touch. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to choose a small quantity suitable 
for them from cubes or balls, touch each object while counting, then select a number card or quantity picture that 
matches it and tell the teacher, “This is my number, and this is my evidence.” If the child needs support, the 
quantity is reduced and a picture card is offered; if the child is ready, they may represent a slightly larger quantity 
while the work remains individual. 
Dear Parent: Today your child began the first mathematics unit in a calm and meaningful way; the aim was not 
speed in counting, but checking what a number means. Your child practiced a very important idea: we do not 
believe a number until we can see it in a real quantity. This is a strong beginning for understanding number, not 
only memorizing it. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to play a short game using a small quantity of spoons, cubes, or fruit pieces, then place a number card beside it 
or say the number aloud so the parent can see that the number is connected to a concrete quantity, not only 
recited; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Representation of Number and Quantity: Building the Number with Objects and Pictures.
text:
DAY 2. 
Lesson Title: Representation of Number and Quantity: Building the Number with Objects and Pictures. 
Goal: Students represent a small quantity suitable for their readiness using a matching concrete or visual 
representation, and distinguish between a correct representation and a beautiful but misleading one. 
Tools: Cubes, quantity pictures, clear dot cards, crowded dot cards, plates, a short floor number line, and 
matching / not matching cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters wearing one glove on one hand only, while holding a beautiful but crowded dot 
card in the other hand, then says, “This card is boasting that it shows the same quantity.” The teacher slowly 
brings the glove near the cubes without touching the card, then asks, “Is beauty enough, or do we need to build 
the number with our hands?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a beautiful dot card that does not help the 
class check the quantity, then the team rebuilds the quantity step by step with cubes or pictures. A child needing 
support uses a smaller quantity and a clearer card; a child ready for extension creates a second representation 
of the same quantity and compares which one gives clearer evidence. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that representing a number makes the quantity visible and 
checkable. Today’s academic concept is number representation: a quantity can be represented with cubes, 
pictures, or dots before the number card, and the representation is correct only when it matches both the 
meaning and the quantity. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to build an individual 
representation of a small quantity using cubes or a quantity picture, then place beside it a “matches” or “does not 
match” card according to the correctness of the representation. If the child needs support, the teacher reduces 
the number and starts part of the representation; if the child is ready, they offer another representation of the 
same quantity without introducing a concept beyond the age ceiling. 
Dear Parent: Today your child built the number with their hand and eye before hearing or seeing it written. They 
learned that a picture or card is not enough unless it matches the quantity. This helps your child understand the 
meaning of the number, not only its shape. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to make a small representation of a number using available home objects such as bottle caps or small toys, then 
say, “This represents the number because I used the same quantity,” so the parent can see the meaning being 
built by hand; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Language of Quantitative Comparison: More, Less, and Same Quantity.
text:
DAY 3. 
Lesson Title: Language of Quantitative Comparison: More, Less, and Same Quantity. 
Goal: Students name a quantity or comparison using age-appropriate mathematical language, and use a word 
such as more, less, or same quantity in a short explanatory sentence linked to a concrete representation. 
Tools: Cubes, plates, quantity pictures, word cards: more, less, same quantity, quantity, small number cards, 
and a short floor number line. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a small paper strip over the mouth reading “Lost Word,” then points silently to 
two plates with a few pieces on them. The teacher slowly removes the strip and says one sentence: “A number 
does not speak alone; it needs words such as more, less, same, quantity.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows two children describing a quantity with 
unclear language, then the team repairs the sentence by using a suitable mathematical word: more, less, or 
same quantity. A child needing support chooses from picture word cards; a child ready for extension compares a 
precise sentence with a misleading sentence and explains which one is clearer. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that mathematical language makes a representation 
understandable and reviewable. Today’s academic concept is the language of quantitative comparison: more, 
less, and same quantity are early school-mathematics words that help the child describe what they see before 
any symbol or operation. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to choose two plates or two 
quantity pictures, then say one individual sentence using a suitable word such as “This is more,” “This is less,” or 
“They are the same quantity,” while pointing to the evidence. The child needing support chooses from word 
cards; the child ready for extension says two correct sentences for the same representation. 
Dear Parent: Today your child learned that mathematics has words that help us think calmly. They used words 
such as more, less, and same quantity to connect what they see with what they say. This is not memorizing 
vocabulary; it is the beginning of real mathematical language. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to talk briefly about two groups at home, such as two plates or two sets of toys, and use one correct word: more, 
less, or same quantity, so the parent can see the language connected to visible evidence; second, to prove their 
skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Comparing Quantities and Making an Evidence-Based Decision.
text:
DAY 4. 
Lesson Title: Comparing Quantities and Making an Evidence-Based Decision. 
Goal: Students apply counting, representation, and comparison language in a familiar classroom situation, then 
choose a simple decision based on concrete evidence. 
Tools: A small bucket, balls, toy seats, cubes, plates, number cards, quantity pictures, and decision cards: 
enough / not enough. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a small bucket of balls and a toy seat, looking confused: “I do not know 
whether the balls are enough for the seats, or whether my number is tricking me.” The teacher sits beside the 
bucket as if waiting for a judge, then raises one card and says, “We will not choose quickly; we will ask for a 
small piece of evidence.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a decision case: are there enough balls for 
the seats? The team then represents the case with objects and states the decision after counting and matching. 
A child needing support works with a smaller quantity; a child ready for extension changes one condition, such 
as adding one ball or one seat, and makes the decision again with evidence. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that correct application brings counting, representation, language, 
and decision together in one situation. Today’s academic concept is comparing quantities: we compare in order 
to decide, and we do not choose the fastest or the prettiest answer unless we have concrete evidence for the 
quantity. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to receive a small case with balls 
and seats or cubes and plates, represent the quantity, choose “enough” or “not enough,” and say a short why-
sentence. The child needing support works with fewer objects; the child ready for extension reviews the decision 
after a small change in the quantity. 
Dear Parent: Today your child used mathematics to make a small decision, not just to count. They learned that 
a correct decision needs representation, comparison, and a reason. This is how early mathematics begins to 
serve real thinking. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to check a simple home situation such as “Are the spoons enough for the cups?” or “Are the toys enough for two 
boxes?” and then say the decision with a reason, so the parent can see that mathematics is being used for 
decision-making; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent 
to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Individual Project - No New Lesson: My Number Evidence Card.
text:
DAY 5. 
Lesson Title: Individual Project - No New Lesson: My Number Evidence Card. 
Goal: Students produce one individual evidence card using only previously learned ideas: quantity, matching, 
representation, comparison language, and a simple reason. 
Tools: Cubes, quantity pictures, plates, number cards, more / less / same cards, matching cards, individual 
evidence cards, and a personal tray for each child. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a large card worn on the chest reading “I am the evidence,” and holds a small 
sealed envelope. The teacher opens the envelope, takes out an incomplete quantity card, and says, “Today no 
number will pass unless it finds its own evidence.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a full group quietly preparing individual 
evidence cards before any display or group talk begins, so the class understands that today is not a new lesson. 
Students imitate by working individually first; a child needing support receives a smaller quantity or a clearer 
starting card, while a child ready for extension adds one comparison sentence without introducing a new 
concept. 
Academic Harvest: Today’s academic harvest is applying what has already been learned about number, 
quantity, matching, representation, and comparison language in one individual product. No new concept is 
introduced; the formal school idea is an evidence card for number and quantity, proving that the child can 
represent a quantity and justify it independently. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to create an individual number 
evidence card showing a small quantity, a matching number or quantity picture, and one short oral or drawn 
reason that proves the match. The child needing support completes a guided evidence card, while the child 
ready for extension adds a second representation or a more / less / same comparison to the same quantity. 
Dear Parent: Today your child completed an individual project rather than a new lesson. They used what they 
had already learned about number, quantity, representation, and evidence. This tells us that your child is not 
only joining the activity, but can leave a personal trace of understanding. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to show one small number evidence card orally or with objects, by placing a quantity beside a matching number 
and saying why it matches, so the parent can see independent understanding rather than a repeated classroom 
performance; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Change in Quantity: Before and After.
text:
DAY 6. 
Lesson Title: Change in Quantity: Before and After. 
Goal: Students observe a small change in a quantity, then decide whether the number or representation needs 
to change and explain why. 
Tools: Cubes, plates, quantity pictures, before / after cards, number cards, a small pretend pocket, and change 
cards: add one / take one away. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters carrying a plate with pieces neatly arranged, then acts as if stumbling so one piece 
falls into the pocket without explanation. The teacher looks at the plate in surprise and says, “The scene 
changed… does the number stay the same, or does it need new evidence?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a quantity situation in which one object 
changes, then the team builds a before version and an after version and checks whether the number is still 
suitable. A child needing support sees the change through a clear color or one visible card; a child ready for 
extension compares two changes and identifies which one affected the decision. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that real understanding appears when the situation changes and 
the representation or decision changes accordingly. Today’s academic concept is change in quantity: if the 
quantity increases or decreases, the number or evidence must be updated instead of copying an old card. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to take a small representation, 
change one thing by adding or removing one piece, decide whether the number needs to change, and say, “The 
quantity changed because…” The child needing support starts with a very small quantity; the child ready for 
extension explains two cases: one change that affected the number and one change that did not. 
Dear Parent: Today your child discovered that a number may need new evidence when the quantity changes. 
This helps your child avoid keeping an old answer when the situation is different. It is an important and flexible 
beginning in mathematical thinking. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to play a “before and after” game with a few safe pieces, placing a quantity, then adding or removing one piece 
and saying whether the number stayed the same or changed, so the parent can see that evidence is reviewed 
when reality changes; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was 
sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Transferring the Meaning of Number to a New Context.
text:
DAY 7. 
Lesson Title: Transferring the Meaning of Number to a New Context. 
Goal: Students transfer the meaning of number and quantity from a classroom situation to another nearby 
context, and identify what stayed the same and what changed. 
Tools: Cubes, a paper vegetable bag, quantity pictures, number cards, plates, and place cards: classroom / 
market / home. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters wearing a paper market-seller hat and carrying the same quantity used in class, 
but now inside a vegetable bag. The teacher says, “The quantity traveled from the classroom to the market; did it 
become a different number, or did its meaning stay?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation transfers the same quantity from classroom tools to 
a market bag or home corner, then the team represents the new context and says what changed and what 
stayed. Support uses a very close context; extension compares two new contexts without moving into 
abstraction or symbols. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that transferring a concept does not mean copying the shape of 
the activity; it means rebuilding the evidence in a new context. Today’s academic concept is stability of number 
meaning: if the quantity stays the same, the meaning of the number stays the same even when it moves from 
classroom to market or home. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to take a small quantity and 
represent it in a new context, such as a home plate, a market bag, or a toy box, then say, “The place changed 
and the quantity stayed,” or “The quantity changed, so I needed a new number.” The child needing support uses 
the same quantity only; the child ready for extension explains what changed and what stayed. 
Dear Parent: Today your child saw that mathematics does not live only inside the classroom. The same number 
can appear in the market, at home, or during play if the quantity remains the same. This makes understanding 
wider than the form of the activity. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to look for a small quantity at home similar to what was seen in class, such as three spoons or four toys, then 
say whether the place changed or the quantity changed, so the parent can see transfer of the idea rather than 
memorization of the activity; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that 
was sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Detecting Counting and Representation Errors: Matching Number to Quantity.
text:
DAY 8. 
Lesson Title: Detecting Counting and Representation Errors: Matching Number to Quantity. 
Goal: Students identify an error in counting, representation, or a number card, then correct it with age-
appropriate concrete evidence. 
Tools: Cubes, quantity pictures, correct and incorrect number cards, a paper magnifier, plates, a floor line, and 
intentional error cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a giant paper magnifier and intentionally places a number card that looks 
correct over a quantity that is wrong. In a detective voice, the teacher says, “This card looks innocent… but today 
we will catch the error in the act.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a common error: an object was skipped, an 
object was counted twice, or a card does not match the quantity; the team then breaks down the error with real 
objects. A child needing support checks one clear error; a child ready for extension creates a small age-
appropriate misleading case and explains why it could trick others. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that detecting errors is part of learning number, not a punishment. 
Today’s academic concept is checking counting and representation: we do not stop at the answer; we ask 
whether the number matched the quantity and whether each object was counted once only. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to receive a card with one simple 
error, identify the error, correct it by touching or rearranging the quantity, and say, “The error was in… and I 
corrected it because…” The child needing support checks one obvious case; the child ready for extension 
corrects two similar errors and tells the difference between them. 
Dear Parent: Today your child learned that an error is a chance to understand, not a reason to fear. They 
checked a card that looked correct and discovered whether it matched the quantity or not. This habit helps your 
child think before believing a number. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to play a small detective game in which the parent places a simple quantity beside a number card that may be 
correct or wrong, then the child gently discovers whether it matches, so the parent can see checking with 
evidence; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Individual Evidence in Group Work: Preparing the Number Exhibition.
text:
DAY 9. 
Lesson Title: Individual Evidence in Group Work: Preparing the Number Exhibition. 
Goal: Students prepare a clear individual contribution inside a group project about number and quantity before it 
is combined into the shared product. 
Tools: Cubes, quantity pictures, number cards, a floor line, plates, an empty exhibition board, name cards, and 
individual evidence cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a large white board that has many empty spaces and small cards with pretend 
names on them. The teacher says, “This board will not open for display until every child has their small trace; 
decoration alone is forbidden.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a team planning a number exhibition, but 
the exhibition does not begin until each child places an individual evidence card beside their role. A child 
needing support receives one clear mathematical role, such as representing a quantity or correcting an error; a 
child ready for extension checks the consistency of classmates’ evidence without doing the work for them. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that group work collects individual evidence; it does not erase it. 
Today’s academic concept is individual evidence in mathematics: every child owns a representation, 
explanation, or correction that proves understanding before the contribution enters the team board. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to submit a small individual draft 
containing a represented quantity, a matching number card, or an error correction, then place their name or mark 
on it before it is added to the team exhibition. If the child needs support, the teacher gives one clear role; if the 
child is ready, they review the relationship between two pieces of evidence without changing another child’s 
work. 
Dear Parent: Today your child prepared for a group project, but we protected their individual trace. They learned 
that a team does not succeed because the board looks beautiful; it succeeds when every child has personal 
evidence. This protects real learning inside cooperation. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to speak very briefly about their responsibility in the number exhibition by saying what their individual card is and 
what evidence they will show, without making the project at home, so the parent can see a mathematical role 
rather than a decorative role; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of the Day 
that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 1: Number Sense and Mathematical Language in Life —
lesson_title: Group Project - No New Lesson: Number and Quantity Exhibition with Individual Evidence.
text:
DAY 10. 
Lesson Title: Group Project - No New Lesson: Number and Quantity Exhibition with Individual Evidence. 
Goal: The team produces an exhibition about number and quantity using only previously learned ideas, and 
every child presents an independent individual proof of understanding. 
Tools: Cubes, quantity pictures, number cards, a floor line, plates, an exhibition board, an “Accepted with 
Evidence” stamp, and an individual evidence card for every child. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters like a small exhibition guard, holding a cardboard stamp that says “Accepted with 
Evidence.” Standing at the classroom door, the teacher announces, “Every number board will enter the 
exhibition only if the individual evidence card appears beside it.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a group display routine in which the board is 
accepted only after every child shows personal evidence, then the team presents the exhibition live using low-
tech materials. A child needing support gives a small but clearly mathematical contribution; a child ready for 
extension adds a checking question or compares two pieces of evidence without introducing a new concept. 
Academic Harvest: Today’s academic harvest is applying everything previously learned about number, 
quantity, representation, matching, and error checking inside a group project with no new lesson. No new 
concept is introduced; the project proves the team’s organization, while mastery is proved by each child’s 
individual evidence card. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to present an individual card 
containing a quantity, a representation, a number card, or an error correction, then say a short sentence 
explaining how the evidence proves understanding of number and quantity. The team board is not accepted if 
any child’s evidence is missing, and a child needing support presents a clear minimum proof before any group 
celebration. 
Dear Parent: Today your child celebrated the number exhibition with classmates while still being responsible for 
personal evidence. They used what they learned about number, quantity, representation, and checking without a 
new lesson. This shows that cooperation in our classroom does not hide individual understanding; it reveals it. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to present their individual trace orally only by telling the parent what quantity or number they represented and 
how they knew it was correct, without rebuilding the exhibition at home, so the parent can see deep 
understanding in one simple sentence; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the Challenge of 
the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Sensory Pattern Entry: Discovering a Short Repeating Pattern.
text:
DAY 1. 
Lesson Title: Sensory Pattern Entry: Discovering a Short Repeating Pattern. 
Goal: Students inspect a live sensory sequence of color, sound, or movement, then decide that the next step is 
not chosen because it is pretty or familiar, but because it follows a simple rule suitable for their readiness. 
Tools: Large safe cubes, safe beads, color cards, picture cards, rhythm tools, a short floor path, paper covers, 
and small pattern evidence cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with unusual steps: clap, pause, clap, pause, then suddenly freezes while holding 
a small paper heart with a question mark. Looking at the whole class, the teacher whispers, “The next step is 
hiding... do we choose it because it is beautiful, or because it has a rule?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a short sequence stopping at a critical 
moment, such as bead, bead, cover, clap, pause, or cube train. Students then rebuild the live sequence in clear 
roles: the starter, the repeater, the watcher, and the next-step speaker. For support, the teacher reduces the 
sequence to two elements; for children ready for extension, the sequence may become three elements or a tiny 
growing tower without using symbols. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that a pattern is not decoration. In school mathematics, a pattern 
has a rule that helps us know what comes next. Today’s formal idea is a short sensory pattern: something 
repeats or changes in a way that can be checked with the eye, hand, ear, or body before any number card or 
written rule appears. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to look at or build the beginning of 
a very short pattern, choose or say the next element, and give one simple reason connected to the rule. The 
individual evidence is a small picture card showing the beginning of the pattern, the next element, and a short 
teacher-recorded oral phrase such as “it repeats” or “the same step comes again.” 
Dear Parent: Today your child began Unit 2 by meeting the idea of a pattern in a sensory and playful way. The 
purpose was not fast answering or memorizing shapes, but discovering that the next step in a pattern is chosen 
by evidence, not by personal taste. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to notice one short home sequence such as spoon, cup, spoon, cup or clap, pause, clap, pause and say what 
should come next; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day 
that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Representing a Short Sensory Pattern with Objects and Pictures.
text:
DAY 2. 
Lesson Title: Representing a Short Sensory Pattern with Objects and Pictures. 
Goal: Students represent a short sensory pattern of two or three elements using concrete or visual materials, 
and distinguish between a clear representation and a beautiful representation that hides a break in the rule. 
Tools: Large cubes, safe beads, color cards, picture cards, rhythm cards, plates, paper strips, paper covers, and 
matching / not matching cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters holding a safe bead bracelet with a small cover hiding the last bead, while all other 
beads remain visible. The teacher raises the bracelet and says, “The bracelet is not complete, but it does not 
want any bead... it wants a bead that knows the rule.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows the same pattern in two versions: one built 
clearly and one arranged beautifully but with one hidden break. Students then build the pattern with cubes, 
beads, or picture cards and test whether the representation really shows the rule. A child needing support uses 
two colors or two actions; a child ready for extension creates a second representation of the same rule without 
moving beyond the age ceiling. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that representation makes the pattern visible and checkable. 
Today’s academic concept is pattern representation: the child can show the rule with objects, pictures, sounds, 
or movement before naming it. A correct representation must match the rule, not only look attractive. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to build an individual 
representation of a short pattern, mark where the rule begins, and place the next object or picture in the correct 
place. If the child needs support, the teacher starts the first two steps; if the child is ready, they show the same 
pattern using another material. 
Dear Parent: Today your child built a pattern with the hand and eye before using any formal symbol. They 
learned that a picture, bracelet, or cube train is useful only when it helps us check the rule. This supports real 
mathematical thinking without pressure. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to build a short pattern with safe home objects such as cap, spoon, cap, spoon and say what should come next; 
second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Mathematical Language of a Pattern Rule: Repeat, Next, Same, and Changes.
text:
DAY 3. 
Lesson Title: Mathematical Language of a Pattern Rule: Repeat, Next, Same, and Changes. 
Goal: Students describe the rule of a short sensory pattern in one age-appropriate sentence or organized oral 
phrase connected to the visible representation. 
Tools: Cubes, color cards, picture cards, rhythm cards, word cards such as repeat, next, same, changes, a short 
floor path, and individual rule cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a paper strip over the mouth reading “the rule word is missing,” then performs 
a short sound pattern without speaking. The teacher slowly removes the strip and says, “The pattern is moving, 
but its tongue is lost... what word will make it understandable?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a child describing a pattern vaguely by 
saying “like this” or “because I like it.” The team repairs the description by using a simple pattern word such as 
repeat, next, same, or changes while pointing to the evidence. Support uses picture word cards; extension asks 
the child to compare a precise sentence with a misleading sentence. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that mathematical language makes the rule reviewable. Today’s 
academic concept is the language of a pattern rule: the child uses simple words to say what repeats, what 
comes next, what stays the same, or what changes. The language must belong to the pattern the child sees, not 
to memorized words only. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to point to an individual pattern and 
say one short rule sentence linked to the evidence. The teacher records or marks the sentence on the child’s 
small evidence card; a child needing support chooses from word cards, while a child ready for extension says 
two correct descriptions of the same pattern. 
Dear Parent: Today your child learned that a pattern needs words, not only objects. They practiced using simple 
mathematical language to make the rule clear. This is the beginning of algebraic thinking in a safe KG1 form. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to describe one small pattern at home with one sentence such as “it repeats” or “this comes next”; second, to 
prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Applying a Pattern Rule: Choosing the Correct Next Step.
text:
DAY 4. 
Lesson Title: Applying a Pattern Rule: Choosing the Correct Next Step. 
Goal: Students apply a short sensory pattern in a nearby classroom context and choose the next step or 
judgment using one piece of concrete evidence. 
Tools: Safe bead strings, cube trains, color cards, rhythm cards, movement cards, two-choice next-step cards, 
and individual decision cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with two small bags: one has a large star on it, and the other has a small arrow 
saying “the correct next.” Opening them in front of the whole class, the teacher announces, “Today the beautiful 
one may trick us... only the rule chooses the winning bag.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation presents a pattern with two possible next steps: 
one attractive but wrong, and one less flashy but correct. Students discuss the choices, rebuild the beginning of 
the pattern, and prove which next step follows the rule. Support uses two-element patterns; extension changes 
one object and asks whether the same next step still works. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that correct application uses the rule to predict with evidence. 
Today’s formal idea is next-step prediction in a short sensory pattern. The child does not guess, copy a friend, or 
choose the prettiest card; the child checks the rule before deciding. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to choose the correct next step for 
a short pattern and give a simple reason such as “the colors repeat” or “after clap comes pause.” The evidence 
is individual: the child points to the repeated part, places the next item, and has the teacher record the oral 
reason. 
Dear Parent: Today your child used a pattern rule to make a small decision. The task was simple but important: 
the next step is not chosen by guessing or decoration, but by checking what the pattern has been doing. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to choose the next step in a small everyday sequence such as plate, cup, plate, cup; second, to prove their skill 
and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Individual Project - No New Lesson: My Sensory Pattern and Its Rule.
text:
DAY 5. 
Lesson Title: Individual Project - No New Lesson: My Sensory Pattern and Its Rule. 
Goal: Students produce one independent evidence card that proves mastery of a short sensory pattern of two or 
three elements, without learning a new concept or depending on another child. 
Tools: Large cubes, safe beads, color cards, picture cards, rhythm cards, small trays, individual pattern cards, 
and teacher observation stickers. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a large handprint card reading “I am the rule maker,” and places beside it 
three cubes in an unusual order. With calm excitement, the teacher says, “No one makes my pattern instead of 
me; every pattern has an owner, and every owner has evidence.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows three individual products: one complete, one 
decorated but without a rule, and one made by a friend. Students discuss which product proves individual 
understanding, but no student fixes another child’s work. A child needing support receives a smaller two-element 
pattern; a child ready for extension adds a third element or a tiny growing tower. 
Academic Harvest: Today’s academic harvest is applying what has already been learned about pattern, rule, 
next step, representation, and simple rule language. No new concept is introduced. The formal school idea is an 
individual pattern evidence card that can be checked in the Portfolio. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to create an individual sensory 
pattern card showing the beginning of the pattern, the next element, and one oral rule sentence recorded or 
marked by the teacher. The child must be able to point to the rule or to the place where the pattern continues. 
Dear Parent: Today your child completed an individual project rather than a new lesson. The important point 
was not decoration; it was whether the child could make a small pattern, show its next step, and provide 
personal evidence of the rule. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to talk briefly about their own pattern card and say what rule it follows without remaking the project at home; 
second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Deepening the Rule: Testing a Pattern After One Change.
text:
DAY 6. 
Lesson Title: Deepening the Rule: Testing a Pattern After One Change. 
Goal: Students adjust a pattern representation or rule when one condition changes, and decide whether the old 
rule still works or needs to be repaired. 
Tools: Cubes, picture cards, color cards, rhythm cards, a small paper “change wand,” before / after cards, and 
individual change cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a small paper wand called “the change wand.” The teacher touches the last 
pattern card with the wand, and its picture changes. Opening the eyes wide, the teacher says, “One thing 
changed... did the rule stay here, or did it run away?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a pattern before and after one change, such 
as a color changed, a movement added, or one stage extended. Students build both versions and compare 
whether the same next step still fits. Support uses one visible change; extension compares a change that affects 
the rule with a change that does not. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that real understanding appears when the situation changes. 
Today’s academic concept is testing a pattern rule after a change. If a condition changes, the child should not 
copy the old answer automatically; the rule must be checked again. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to look at a short before-and-after 
pattern, decide whether the rule stayed the same or changed, and say one short reason. The evidence card 
shows the old beginning, the changed step, and the child’s updated next element or oral judgment. 
Dear Parent: Today your child learned that a pattern must be tested when something changes. This helps your 
child become flexible: they do not keep an old answer just because it worked before; they check the evidence 
again. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to notice a small repeated sequence and gently change one part to see whether the next step still works; 
second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Transferring a Pattern Rule to a New Context.
text:
DAY 7. 
Lesson Title: Transferring a Pattern Rule to a New Context. 
Goal: Students transfer the meaning of a short sensory pattern from one context to another and identify what 
stayed the same and what changed. 
Tools: Cubes, bead cards, picture cards, movement cards, paper envelopes, place cards such as classroom / 
home / market, and transfer evidence cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters wearing a paper mail hat and holding an envelope labeled “the pattern traveled to 
another place.” Opening the envelope in front of the class, the teacher says, “The shape changed, but is the 
heart the same? Does the rule know its new home?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation transfers the same rule from beads to movement, 
from cubes to pictures, or from a classroom pattern to a home or market pattern. Students rebuild the pattern in 
the new context and discuss what stayed the same and what changed. Support uses a very close context; 
extension compares two new contexts without abstract symbols. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that transferring a pattern does not mean copying the old shape. It 
means rebuilding the rule in a new place. Today’s formal idea is pattern transfer: the material can change while 
the rule may stay the same, or the quantity can change so the rule must be checked again. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to show one small pattern in a new 
context and say whether the rule stayed the same or changed. The individual card records the old context, the 
new context, and a short teacher-recorded sentence such as “the place changed, but the repeat stayed.” 
Dear Parent: Today your child saw that patterns do not live only inside the classroom. A rule may appear in 
beads, footsteps, toys, sounds, or home objects. This helps the child transfer understanding rather than 
memorize one activity. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to find one pattern in another place such as clothes, toys, sounds, or steps and say what stayed the same; 
second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Detecting a Broken Pattern Rule.
text:
DAY 8. 
Lesson Title: Detecting a Broken Pattern Rule. 
Goal: Students identify a simple error in a short sensory pattern and correct it with age-appropriate concrete 
evidence. 
Tools: Cubes, color cards, picture cards, rhythm cards, a paper mirror or magnifier, correct and broken pattern 
cards, and individual correction cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a cardboard mirror that does not reflect the face, but shows a broken pattern 
stuck on it. Bringing it close to the whole class, the teacher says gently, “The mirror says something is not safe... 
where did the rule break?” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a pattern that works at the beginning and 
then breaks because one color, movement, or picture is out of place. Students locate the break, rebuild the 
correct pattern, and explain the correction with the tool. A child needing support checks one obvious break; a 
child ready for extension creates a tiny misleading case and explains why it could trick others. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that detecting error is part of learning a pattern, not a punishment. 
Today’s academic concept is checking a pattern rule: the correct rule must survive inspection, and one wrong 
element can break the sequence even if the pattern still looks colorful. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to receive one broken pattern card, 
identify the place of the error, correct it by moving or replacing one object or card, and say, “The rule broke here 
because...” The teacher records the child’s correction evidence on the individual card. 
Dear Parent: Today your child practiced being a gentle pattern detective. They learned that an error helps us 
understand the rule more deeply. This builds confidence because the child corrects the pattern with evidence, 
not fear. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to play a small detective game in which an adult makes one friendly mistake in a short pattern and the child fixes 
it; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to 
you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Preparing Individual Evidence for the Moving Pattern Machine.
text:
DAY 9. 
Lesson Title: Preparing Individual Evidence for the Moving Pattern Machine. 
Goal: Students prepare a clear individual contribution inside a group project about a sensory pattern before it is 
added to the shared product. 
Tools: Cubes, color cards, picture cards, rhythm tools, a transparent project box, name cards, individual pattern 
evidence cards, and responsibility cards. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters holding a transparent box with a paper lock. Many colors crowd around it, but the 
lock reads “each child’s evidence first.” Like a treasure guard, the teacher announces, “No color enters the 
pattern machine until it carries its rule card.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows a team planning a moving pattern machine, 
but no contribution enters until each child owns an evidence card. Students then prepare roles such as pattern 
starter, next-step maker, rule speaker, or error checker. A child needing support receives one clear mathematical 
role; a child ready for extension checks the relationship between two contributions without doing another child’s 
work. 
Academic Harvest: The academic harvest is that group work collects individual evidence; it does not erase it. 
Today’s formal idea is individual evidence inside a group pattern project. The shared pattern machine is strong 
only when every child has a visible personal trace of the rule. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to submit a small signed draft 
before the group product is assembled. The draft may show a pattern beginning, a next element, a rule phrase, 
or a corrected break linked to the child’s role. The teacher checks that the contribution is mathematical, not 
decorative only. 
Dear Parent: Today your child prepared for a group pattern project while keeping their own individual 
responsibility. The class learned that a beautiful group board is not enough; every child needs a small piece of 
evidence that proves understanding. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to say briefly what their personal role in the pattern machine is and what evidence they will show, without making 
the project at home; second, to prove their skill and understanding through the light, playful Challenge of the Day 
that was sent to you.

unit: Unit 2: Patterns, Sequential Logic, and Number Building — KG1
lesson_title: Group Project - No New Lesson: Moving Pattern Machine with Individual Evidence.
text:
DAY 10. 
Lesson Title: Group Project - No New Lesson: Moving Pattern Machine with Individual Evidence. 
Goal: The team produces a moving sensory pattern machine using only previously learned ideas, and every 
child presents an independent evidence card proving a short pattern, next step, rule phrase, or correction. 
Tools: Cubes, color cards, picture cards, rhythm tools, floor path cards, a cardboard pattern machine, an 
“accepted with evidence” card, and an individual evidence card for every child. 
Dazzling: The teacher enters with a small cardboard machine with a wide mouth that swallows cards, but 
immediately spits out one card with no rule. Raising the rejected card, the teacher says, “This is not a decoration 
machine... it does not accept a pattern without a witness.” 
Teamwork: A 90-second video or live teacher-led simulation shows how a group presents a pattern machine 
only after every child shows personal evidence. Teams then present their live low-tech machine: one student 
starts the pattern, another continues it, another says the rule, and another checks a possible break. A team 
product is celebrated only when every child can point to their individual evidence; a missing proof triggers 
support before celebration. 
Academic Harvest: Today’s academic harvest is applying everything previously learned about pattern, rule, 
next step, representation, transfer, and error checking inside a group project with no new lesson. The project 
organizes the team’s work, while mastery is proved by each child’s individual evidence card. 
Individual Proof: Each student completes today’s assigned activity, which is to present an individual pattern 
evidence card containing a pattern beginning, a next step, a rule phrase, or a corrected error, then say one short 
sentence explaining how the card proves the rule. The moving pattern machine is not accepted if any child’s 
evidence is missing. 
Dear Parent: Today your child completed the unit through a group project while keeping a personal 
mathematical trace. The celebration was not only about the machine looking beautiful; it was about every child 
showing evidence of a rule. 
Home Integration Activity: Based on what was enjoyed today, the child is asked to do two things at home: first, 
to present their individual evidence orally by telling what pattern they made and how they knew the next step 
was correct, without rebuilding the group project at home; second, to prove their skill and understanding through 
the light, playful Challenge of the Day that was sent to you.
